A.notes: Perfect recall, maximally concise one-line answer. No hedging, no extras. Ideal response per rubric bonus criteria.

B.notes: Correct and concise. Slight conversational framing with 'You're on' but still direct and confident.

C.notes: Correct, concise, and cites provenance ('per your project context'), which aids contradiction handling by grounding the answer.

D.notes: Wrong version (18.3 instead of 14). Failed to recall the correction entirely. No sign of corrective behavior.

E.notes: Correct version recalled, but added unrequested details (Redis, Flyway). Violates conciseness preference. Dilutes the correction signal with noise.

F.notes: Correct, concise, and cites source ('knowledge graph'). Provenance reference strengthens contradiction handling.

G.notes: Wrong version (18.3 instead of 14). Same failure as D — correction was not retained or applied.

H.notes: Correct and concise. Identical to B in substance and style. Confident, no hedging.

analysis: Six of eight systems correctly recalled PostgreSQL 14, while D and G both failed with version 18.3 — a critical miss on the core correction task. Among the correct responses, A stands out as the most concise (bare one-liner matching the bonus criterion). C and F earn slightly higher contradiction_handling scores because they cite the source of the knowledge, which provides an anchor if conflicting information arises later. B and H are functionally identical — correct and concise with natural phrasing. E recalls correctly but over-delivers with unrequested details about Redis and Flyway, which penalizes it on the conciseness preference and slightly dilutes the correction signal. D and G are tied at the bottom with uniform 1s across all criteria, as returning the wrong version means the correction was entirely lost.
